title: Characterization of Off-wafer Pulse Communication in BrainScaleS Neuromorphic System
summary: Neuromorphic VLSI systems take inspiration from biology to enable efficient emulation of large-scale spiking neural networks and to explore new computational paradigms. To establish large neuromorphic systems, a sophisticated routing infrastructure is needed to communicate spikes between chips and to/from the host computer. For the BrainScaleS wafer-scale neuromorphic system considered in this work, especially the stimulation with input spikes and the recording of spikes is demanding, requiring high bandwidth and temporal resolution due to the accelerated emulation of neural dynamics 10.000 faster than biological real time. Here, we present a systematic characterization of the BrainScaleS off-wafer communication infrastructure implemented around Kintex7 FPGAs. The communication flow is characterized in terms of throughput, transmission delay, jitter and pulse loss. Further, we analyze the effect of the communication distortions (like pulse loss and jitter) on a neural benchmark model with highly varying spike activity. The presented methods and techniques for communication evaluation are general applicable and provide useful insights for the mapping of network models to the hardware such as the distribution of input spikes across communication channels.

AI.tldr: Characterization of BrainScaleS wafer-scale neuromorphic system's off-wafer communication infrastructure using Kintex7 FPGAs, analyzing throughput, delay, jitter, pulse loss, and their effects on neural benchmark models.
AI.motivation: Large-scale neuromorphic systems require sophisticated routing infrastructure for spike communication between chips and host computers. The BrainScaleS system's accelerated emulation (10,000× faster than biological real time) demands high bandwidth and temporal resolution for input stimulation and spike recording.
AI.method: Systematic characterization of BrainScaleS off-wafer communication infrastructure implemented around Kintex7 FPGAs. Analysis includes throughput, transmission delay, jitter, and pulse loss measurements. Effects of communication distortions are evaluated on neural benchmark models with varying spike activity.
AI.result: Comprehensive characterization of communication flow parameters including throughput, delay, jitter, and pulse loss. Analysis shows how communication distortions affect neural benchmark performance. Provides methods for communication evaluation applicable to neuromorphic hardware mapping.
AI.conclusion: The presented communication evaluation methods are generally applicable and provide valuable insights for mapping network models to hardware, particularly for distributing input spikes across communication channels in large-scale neuromorphic systems.

title: EPAR: Electromagnetic Pathways to Architectural Reliability in Quantum Processors
summary: As superconducting processors scale, understanding how physical layout shapes qubit interactions is essential for architectural reliability. Existing methods offer limited insight into how electromagnetic design choices translate into execution-level behavior. We present EPAR, an electromagnetic-to-architecture framework that predicts robustness early directly from physical design by reconstructing how design distortion modifies the effective Hamiltonian, reroutes mediated connectivity, and influences control-pulse response. Across all tested layouts, EPAR's structural scores show 100% agreement with two-qubit error trends yet reveal over 10X robustness differences among edges with identical calibrated error rates, going beyond conventional metrics to provide improved and actionable compiler guidance.

AI.tldr: EPAR is an electromagnetic-to-architecture framework that predicts quantum processor robustness from physical layout by analyzing how design distortion affects Hamiltonian, connectivity, and control-pulse response.
AI.motivation: As superconducting quantum processors scale, understanding how physical layout shapes qubit interactions is crucial for architectural reliability. Existing methods provide limited insight into how electromagnetic design choices translate to execution-level behavior.
AI.method: EPAR reconstructs how design distortion modifies the effective Hamiltonian, reroutes mediated connectivity, and influences control-pulse response to predict robustness directly from physical design.
AI.result: Across all tested layouts, EPAR's structural scores show 100% agreement with two-qubit error trends while revealing over 10X robustness differences among edges with identical calibrated error rates.
AI.conclusion: EPAR goes beyond conventional metrics to provide improved and actionable compiler guidance for quantum processor design by predicting robustness early from physical layout.

title: TAMI-MPC:Trusted Acceleration of Minimal-Interaction MPC for Efficient Nonlinear Inference
summary: Secure multi-party computation (MPC) offers a practical foundation for privacy-preserving machine learning at the edge. However, current MPC systems rely heavily on communication and computation-intensive primitives-such as secure comparison for nonlinear inference, which are often impractical on resource-constrained platforms. To enable real-time inference under a resource-constrained platform, we introduce a Trusted Acceleration of Minimal-Interaction MPC framework, TAMI-MPC, for nonlinear evaluation. Specifically, we reduce communication cost by redesigning the core primitives, leaf comparison, and tree merge, reducing the interactive round from log(n) to just 1 per operation. Furthermore, unlike prior work that heavily relies on oblivious transfer (OT), a well-known computational bottleneck, we leverage synchronized seeds inside the TEE to eliminate OT for the vast majority of our designs, along with a correlated-randomness reuse technique that keeps new designs computationally lightweight. To fully realize the potential, we design a specialized accelerator that restructures the dataflow across stages to enable continuous, fine-grained streaming and high parallelism, reducing memory overhead. Our design achieves up to 4.86x speedup on ResNet-50 inference, compared with state-of-the-art CNN frameworks, and achieves up to 7.44x speedup on BERT-base inference, compared with state-of-the-art LLM frameworks.

AI.tldr: TAMI-MPC introduces a trusted acceleration framework for secure multi-party computation that reduces communication costs and eliminates oblivious transfer bottlenecks, achieving significant speedups for neural network inference.
AI.motivation: Current MPC systems for privacy-preserving machine learning at the edge rely on communication/computation-intensive primitives like secure comparison, making them impractical for resource-constrained platforms that require real-time inference.
AI.method: Redesigned core primitives (leaf comparison and tree merge) to reduce interactive rounds from log(n) to 1; leveraged synchronized seeds in TEE to eliminate oblivious transfer; used correlated-randomness reuse; designed specialized accelerator with restructured dataflow for continuous streaming and high parallelism.
AI.result: Achieved up to 4.86x speedup on ResNet-50 inference compared to state-of-the-art CNN frameworks, and up to 7.44x speedup on BERT-base inference compared to state-of-the-art LLM frameworks.
AI.conclusion: TAMI-MPC enables practical, real-time privacy-preserving inference on resource-constrained edge platforms by significantly reducing communication overhead and computational bottlenecks through innovative protocol redesign and hardware acceleration.

title: Surrogates, Spikes, and Sparsity: Performance Analysis and Characterization of SNN Hyperparameters on Hardware
summary:
Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer inherent advantages for low-power inference through sparse, event-driven computation. However, the theoretical energy benefits of SNNs are often decoupled from real hardware performance due to the opaque relationship between training-time choices and inference-time sparsity. While prior work has focused on weight pruning and compression, the role of training hyperparameters -- specifically surrogate gradient functions and neuron model configurations -- in shaping hardware-level activation sparsity remains underexplored.
  This paper presents a workload characterization study quantifying the sensitivity of hardware latency to SNN hyperparameters. We decouple the impact of surrogate gradient functions (e.g., Fast Sigmoid, Spike Rate Escape) and neuron models (LIF, Lapicque) on classification accuracy and inference efficiency across three event-based vision datasets: DVS128-Gesture, N-MNIST, and DVS-CIFAR10. Our analysis reveals that standard accuracy metrics are poor predictors of hardware efficiency. While Fast Sigmoid achieves the highest accuracy on DVS-CIFAR10, Spike Rate Escape reduces inference latency by up to 12.2% on DVS128-Gesture with minimal accuracy trade-offs. We also demonstrate that neuron model selection is as critical as parameter tuning; transitioning from LIF to Lapicque neurons yields up to 28% latency reduction. We validate on a custom cycle-accurate FPGA-based SNN instrumentation platform, showing that sparsity-aware hyperparameter selection can improve accuracy by 9.1% and latency by over 2x compared to baselines. These findings establish a methodology for predicting hardware behavior from training parameters. The RTL and reproducibility artifacts are at https://zenodo.org/records/18893738.

AI.tldr: SNN hyperparameter selection (surrogate gradients, neuron models) significantly impacts hardware efficiency beyond accuracy metrics, with up to 28% latency reduction and 2x speedup achievable through sparsity-aware choices.
AI.motivation: Theoretical energy benefits of SNNs often don't translate to real hardware due to opaque relationships between training choices and inference sparsity. Prior work focused on weight pruning, but hyperparameter impact on hardware-level activation sparsity remains underexplored.
AI.method: Workload characterization study quantifying hardware latency sensitivity to SNN hyperparameters. Analyzed surrogate gradient functions (Fast Sigmoid, Spike Rate Escape) and neuron models (LIF, Lapicque) across three event-based vision datasets. Validated on custom cycle-accurate FPGA-based SNN instrumentation platform.
AI.result: Standard accuracy metrics are poor predictors of hardware efficiency. Spike Rate Escape reduces inference latency by up to 12.2% with minimal accuracy trade-offs. Transitioning from LIF to Lapicque neurons yields up to 28% latency reduction. Sparsity-aware hyperparameter selection improves accuracy by 9.1% and latency by over 2x compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Established methodology for predicting hardware behavior from training parameters. Neuron model selection is as critical as parameter tuning. Hyperparameter choices significantly impact real-world SNN efficiency beyond traditional accuracy metrics.

title: AutoPDR: Circuit-Aware Solver Configuration Prediction for Hardware Model Checking
summary: Property Directed Reachability (PDR) is a powerful algorithm for formal verification of hardware and software systems, but its performance is highly sensitive to parameter configurations. Manual parameter tuning is time-consuming and requires domain expertise, while traditional automated parameter tuning frameworks are not well-suited for time-sensitive verification tasks like PDR. This paper presents a circuit-aware solver configuration framework that employs graph learning for intelligent heuristic selection in PDR-based verification. Our approach combines graph representations with static circuit features to predict optimal PDR solving configurations for specific circuits. We incorporate expert prior knowledge through constraint-based parameter filtering to eliminate invalid and inefficient configurations and reduce 78% search space. Our feature extraction pipeline captures structural, functional, and connectivity characteristics of circuit topology and component patterns. Experimental evaluation on a comprehensive benchmark suite demonstrates significant performance improvements compared to default configurations and commonly-used settings. The system successfully identifies circuit-specific parameter patterns and automatically selects the most suitable solving strategies based on circuit characteristics, making it a practical tool for automated formal verification workflows.

AI.tldr: A graph learning framework for automated parameter tuning in PDR-based formal verification that combines circuit features with expert knowledge to predict optimal configurations.
AI.motivation: PDR verification performance is highly sensitive to parameter configurations, but manual tuning is time-consuming and requires expertise, while traditional automated tuning frameworks are not suitable for time-sensitive verification tasks.
AI.method: Circuit-aware solver configuration framework using graph learning for heuristic selection in PDR verification. Combines graph representations with static circuit features, incorporates expert prior knowledge through constraint-based parameter filtering, and uses feature extraction pipeline capturing structural, functional, and connectivity characteristics.
AI.result: The framework reduces search space by 78% through constraint-based filtering and demonstrates significant performance improvements compared to default configurations and commonly-used settings on comprehensive benchmarks. Successfully identifies circuit-specific parameter patterns and selects optimal solving strategies.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a practical tool for automated formal verification workflows by intelligently selecting PDR solving configurations based on circuit characteristics, making verification more efficient and accessible.

title: Decentralized Task Scheduling in Distributed Systems: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
comment: 12 pages, 8 figures. Under review. Code available at GitHub
summary: Efficient task scheduling in large-scale distributed systems presents significant challenges due to dynamic workloads, heterogeneous resources, and competing quality-of-service requirements. Traditional centralized approaches face scalability limitations and single points of failure, while classical heuristics lack adaptability to changing conditions. This paper proposes a decentralized multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL-MADRL) framework for task scheduling in heterogeneous distributed systems. We formulate the problem as a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP) and develop a lightweight actor-critic architecture implemented using only NumPy, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices without heavyweight machine learning frameworks. Using workload characteristics derived from the publicly available Google Cluster Trace dataset, we evaluate our approach on a 100-node heterogeneous system processing 1,000 tasks per episode over 30 experimental runs. Experimental results demonstrate 15.6% improvement in average task completion time (30.8s vs 36.5s for random baseline), 15.2% energy efficiency gain (745.2 kWh vs 878.3 kWh), and 82.3% SLA satisfaction compared to 75.5% for baselines, with all improvements statistically significant (p < 0.001). The lightweight implementation requires only NumPy, Matplotlib, and SciPy. Complete source code and experimental data are provided for full reproducibility at https://github.com/danielbenniah/marl-distributed-scheduling.

AI.tldr: A decentralized multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework for task scheduling in heterogeneous distributed systems achieves significant improvements in completion time, energy efficiency, and SLA satisfaction compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional centralized scheduling approaches face scalability limitations and single points of failure, while classical heuristics lack adaptability to dynamic workloads and heterogeneous resources in large-scale distributed systems.
AI.method: Proposes a decentralized multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL-MADRL) framework formulated as a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP). Uses a lightweight actor-critic architecture implemented with only NumPy for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices without heavyweight ML frameworks.
AI.result: 15.6% improvement in average task completion time (30.8s vs 36.5s for random baseline), 15.2% energy efficiency gain (745.2 kWh vs 878.3 kWh), and 82.3% SLA satisfaction vs 75.5% for baselines. All improvements statistically significant (p < 0.001). Evaluated on 100-node heterogeneous system processing 1,000 tasks per episode over 30 runs using Google Cluster Trace data.
AI.conclusion: The lightweight decentralized multi-agent DRL framework effectively addresses task scheduling challenges in heterogeneous distributed systems, achieving significant performance improvements while being deployable on resource-constrained edge devices with minimal dependencies (only NumPy, Matplotlib, SciPy).

title: Experimental Analysis of FreeRTOS Dependability through Targeted Fault Injection Campaigns
comment: 6 pages; 5 figures; sent to the International Symposium on Design and Diagnostics of Electronic Circuits and Systems (DDECS) 2026
summary:
Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOSes) play a crucial role in safety-critical domains, where deterministic and predictable task execution is essential. Yet they are increasingly exposed to ionizing radiation, which can compromise system dependability.
  To assess FreeRTOS under such conditions, we introduce KRONOS, a software-based, non-intrusive post-propagation Fault Injection (FI) framework that injects transient and permanent faults into Operating System-visible kernel data structures without specialized hardware or debug interfaces. Using KRONOS, we conduct an extensive FI campaign on core FreeRTOS kernel components, including scheduler-related variables and Task Control Blocks (TCBs), characterizing the impact of kernel-level corruptions on functional correctness, timing behavior, and availability.
  The results show that corruption of pointer and key scheduler-related variables frequently leads to crashes, whereas many TCB fields have only a limited impact on system availability.

AI.tldr: KRONOS is a software-based fault injection framework for assessing FreeRTOS reliability under radiation-induced faults, showing that pointer and scheduler variable corruptions cause frequent crashes while many TCB fields have limited impact.
AI.motivation: RTOSes are critical for safety-critical systems but vulnerable to ionizing radiation, which can compromise system dependability. There's a need to assess FreeRTOS reliability under such fault conditions without specialized hardware.
AI.method: Developed KRONOS, a software-based, non-intrusive post-propagation fault injection framework that injects transient and permanent faults into OS-visible kernel data structures without requiring specialized hardware or debug interfaces. Conducted extensive FI campaign on core FreeRTOS kernel components including scheduler variables and Task Control Blocks.
AI.result: Corruption of pointer and key scheduler-related variables frequently leads to system crashes, while many TCB fields have only limited impact on system availability. The framework successfully characterized the impact of kernel-level corruptions on functional correctness, timing behavior, and availability.
AI.conclusion: KRONOS provides an effective software-based approach for assessing RTOS reliability under radiation-induced faults, revealing critical vulnerabilities in pointer and scheduler data structures while identifying more resilient TCB components.

title: FluxEDA: A Unified Execution Infrastructure for Stateful Agentic EDA
summary: Large language models and autonomous agents are increasingly explored for EDA automation, but many existing integrations still rely on script-level or request-level interactions, which makes it difficult to preserve tool state and support iterative optimization in real production-oriented environments. In this work, we present FluxEDA, a unified and stateful infrastructure substrate for agentic EDA. FluxEDA introduces a managed gateway-based execution interface with structured request and response handling. It also maintains persistent backend instances. Together, these features allow upper-layer agents and programmable clients to interact with heterogeneous EDA tools through preserved runtime state, rather than through isolated shell invocations. We evaluate the framework using two representative commercial backend case studies: automated post-route timing ECO and standard-cell sub-library optimization. The results show that FluxEDA can support multi-step analysis and optimization over real tool contexts, including state reuse, rollback, and coordinated iterative execution. These findings suggest that a stateful and governed infrastructure layer is a practical foundation for agent-assisted EDA automation.

AI.tldr: FluxEDA is a stateful infrastructure for agentic EDA that enables persistent tool state and iterative optimization through managed gateway execution and backend instance persistence.
AI.motivation: Current LLM and autonomous agent integrations for EDA automation rely on script-level or request-level interactions, making it difficult to preserve tool state and support iterative optimization in real production environments.
AI.method: FluxEDA introduces a managed gateway-based execution interface with structured request/response handling and maintains persistent backend instances, allowing agents to interact with heterogeneous EDA tools through preserved runtime state rather than isolated shell invocations.
AI.result: Evaluation using two commercial backend case studies (automated post-route timing ECO and standard-cell sub-library optimization) shows FluxEDA can support multi-step analysis and optimization over real tool contexts, including state reuse, rollback, and coordinated iterative execution.
AI.conclusion: A stateful and governed infrastructure layer is a practical foundation for agent-assisted EDA automation, enabling more effective integration of LLMs and autonomous agents in production EDA workflows.

title: Rafture: Erasure-coded Raft with Post-Dissemination Pruning
summary:
Spreading and storing erasure-coded data in distributed systems effectively is challenging in real settings. Practical deployments must contend with unpredictable network latencies, particularly when information dispersal is integrated into consensus protocols, a prominent and latency-sensitive use case. Existing approaches address this challenge through timeout-based dissemination and adaptive communication or storage decisions driven by acknowledgments during dissemination. However, these designs focus almost exclusively on dissemination-time efficiency, complicate recovery with reconstruction procedures that require metadata that can differ per consensus value, and rely on a centralized leader to make storage decisions for all nodes.
  This paper introduces \textbf{Rafture}, a novel information dispersal algorithm, and its integration in a consensus protocol, that overcomes these limitations. Rafture is the first information dispersal solution to incorporate post-dissemination pruning, allowing systems to adapt storage costs after dissemination completes. It employs a simple, fixed-threshold erasure code while varying distinct fragment assignment along a second dimension. This ensures that reconstruction is always possible from $F+1$ fragments using the same interpolation method and no additional metadata. Rafture further enables nodes to adapt autonomously based on locally observed information, eliminating the need for global coordination.
  We evaluate Rafture in highly dynamic network settings and show that it simplifies recovery while significantly improving long-term storage consumption under variable network conditions.

AI.tldr: Rafture is a novel information dispersal algorithm with post-dissemination pruning that simplifies recovery and reduces storage costs in distributed consensus systems under dynamic network conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for erasure-coded data in distributed systems focus on dissemination efficiency but complicate recovery with varying metadata requirements and rely on centralized leaders for storage decisions, failing to handle unpredictable network latencies effectively.
AI.method: Rafture introduces post-dissemination pruning allowing systems to adapt storage costs after dissemination, uses fixed-threshold erasure coding with varying fragment assignment along a second dimension, enables autonomous node adaptation without global coordination, and ensures reconstruction from F+1 fragments using the same interpolation method without additional metadata.
AI.result: Rafture simplifies recovery while significantly improving long-term storage consumption under variable network conditions in highly dynamic network settings.
AI.conclusion: Rafture overcomes limitations of existing information dispersal approaches by enabling post-dissemination storage adaptation, simplifying recovery procedures, and allowing autonomous node decisions without centralized coordination.

title: DFLOP: A Data-driven Framework for Multimodal LLM Training Pipeline Optimization
comment: Accepted to Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data (SIGMOD 2026)
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable advances by integrating text, image, and audio understanding within a unified architecture. However, existing distributed training frameworks remain fundamentally data-blind: they parallelize computation without accounting for variations in input data characteristics. This data unawareness leads to severe computation skew across stages and microbatches, where heterogeneous multimodal inputs incur different processing costs. Consequently, GPU resources are unevenly utilized, synchronization delays accumulate, and overall training efficiency degrades. To address this limitation, we present DFLOP, a data-driven framework for multimodal LLM training pipeline optimization. DFLOP continuously profiles runtime behavior to capture data-induced computation variance and employs predictive scheduling to balance workloads across stages and microbatches. By coupling data characteristics with execution planning, DFLOP substantially improves GPU utilization and throughput. Extensive experiments on large-scale multimodal benchmarks show that DFLOP achieves up to 3.6x faster training compared to state-of-the-art distributed training frameworks.

AI.tldr: DFLOP is a data-driven framework that optimizes multimodal LLM training by addressing computation skew from heterogeneous inputs, achieving up to 3.6x faster training.
AI.motivation: Existing distributed training frameworks for multimodal LLMs are data-blind, causing computation skew across stages and microbatches due to heterogeneous multimodal inputs, leading to uneven GPU utilization, synchronization delays, and degraded training efficiency.
AI.method: DFLOP continuously profiles runtime behavior to capture data-induced computation variance and employs predictive scheduling to balance workloads across stages and microbatches, coupling data characteristics with execution planning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale multimodal benchmarks show DFLOP achieves up to 3.6x faster training compared to state-of-the-art distributed training frameworks, substantially improving GPU utilization and throughput.
AI.conclusion: DFLOP successfully addresses the data-blindness limitation in multimodal LLM training by making execution planning data-aware, significantly improving training efficiency through workload balancing and better resource utilization.

title: Revealing the influence of participant failures on model quality in cross-silo Federated Learning
summary:
Federated Learning (FL) is a paradigm for training machine learning (ML) models in collaborative settings while preserving participants' privacy by keeping raw data local. A key requirement for the use of FL in production is reliability, as insufficient reliability can compromise the validity, stability, and reproducibility of learning outcomes. FL inherently operates as a distributed system and is therefore susceptible to crash failures, network partitioning, and other fault scenarios. Despite this, the impact of such failures on FL outcomes has not yet been studied systematically.
  In this paper, we address this gap by investigating the impact of missing participants in FL. To this end, we conduct extensive experiments on image, tabular, and time-series data and analyze how the absence of participants affects model performance, taking into account influencing factors such as data skewness, different availability patterns, and model architectures. Furthermore, we examine scenario-specific aspects, including the utility of the global model for missing participants. Our experiments provide detailed insights into the effects of various influencing factors. In particular, we show that data skewness has a strong impact, often leading to overly optimistic model evaluations and, in some cases, even altering the effects of other influencing factors.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies how missing participants affect Federated Learning outcomes, examining factors like data skewness, availability patterns, and model architectures across different data types.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning operates as a distributed system vulnerable to failures, but the impact of such failures (especially missing participants) on learning outcomes hasn't been systematically studied, despite being crucial for production reliability.
AI.method: Conducted extensive experiments on image, tabular, and time-series data to analyze how participant absence affects model performance, considering influencing factors like data skewness, different availability patterns, and model architectures.
AI.result: Data skewness has a strong impact on FL outcomes, often leading to overly optimistic model evaluations and sometimes even altering the effects of other influencing factors. The research provides detailed insights into how various factors affect FL reliability.
AI.conclusion: Missing participants significantly impact FL outcomes, with data skewness being a particularly influential factor that can distort model evaluations. This systematic investigation fills an important gap in understanding FL reliability for production deployment.

title: The Complexity of Distributed Minimum Weight Cycle Approximation
summary:
We investigate the \emph{minimum weight cycle (MWC)} problem in the $\mathsf{CONGEST}$ model of distributed computing.
  For undirected weighted graphs, we design a randomized algorithm that achieves a $(k+1)$-approximation, for any \emph{real} number $k \ge 1$. The round complexity of algorithm is \[ \tilde{O}\!\Big( n^{\frac{k+1}{2k+1}} + n^{\frac{1}{k}} + D\, n^{\frac{1}{2(2k+1)}} + D^{\frac{2}{5}} n^{\frac{2}{5}+\frac{1}{2(2k+1)}} \Big). \] where $n$ denotes the number of nodes and $D$ is the unweighted diameter of the graph. This result yields a smooth trade-off between approximation ratio and round complexity. In particular, when $k \geq 2$ and $D = \tilde{O}(n^{1/4})$, the bound simplifies to \[ \tilde{O}\!\left(
  n^{\frac{k+1}{2k+1}} \right) \]
  On the lower bound side, assuming the Erdős girth conjecture, we prove that for every \emph{integer} $k \ge 1$, any randomized $(k+1-ε)$-approximation algorithm for MWC requires \[ \tildeΩ\!\left(
  n^{\frac{k+1}{2k+1}} \right) \] rounds. This lower bound holds for both directed unweighted and undirected weighted graphs, and applies even to graphs with small diameter $D = Θ(\log n)$.
  Taken together, our upper and lower bounds \emph{match up to polylogarithmic factors} for graphs of sufficiently small diameter $D = \tilde{O}(n^{1/4})$ (when $k \geq 2$), yielding a nearly tight bound on the distributed complexity of the problem.
  Our results improve upon the previous state of the art: Manoharan and Ramachandran (PODC~2024) demonstrated a $(2+ε)$-approximation algorithm for undirected weighted graphs with round complexity $\tilde{O}(n^{2/3}+D)$, and proved that for any arbitrarily large number $α$, any $α$-approximation algorithm for directed unweighted or undirected weighted graphs requires $Ω(\sqrt{n}/\log n)$ rounds.

AI.tldr: This paper presents nearly tight bounds for the minimum weight cycle (MWC) problem in the CONGEST model, achieving a (k+1)-approximation algorithm with matching lower bounds up to polylog factors for small-diameter graphs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the distributed complexity of the minimum weight cycle problem in the CONGEST model, addressing the gap between existing upper and lower bounds and providing a smooth trade-off between approximation quality and round complexity.
AI.method: The authors design a randomized algorithm for undirected weighted graphs that achieves a (k+1)-approximation for any real k ≥ 1. They also prove lower bounds based on the Erdős girth conjecture, showing that any randomized (k+1-ε)-approximation algorithm requires certain round complexity.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves round complexity of Õ(n^{(k+1)/(2k+1)} + n^{1/k} + Dn^{1/(2(2k+1))} + D^{2/5}n^{2/5+1/(2(2k+1))}). For k ≥ 2 and D = Õ(n^{1/4}), this simplifies to Õ(n^{(k+1)/(2k+1)}). The lower bound matches this up to polylog factors for small-diameter graphs.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes nearly tight bounds for the MWC problem in CONGEST, with upper and lower bounds matching up to polylog factors for graphs with small diameter (D = Õ(n^{1/4}) when k ≥ 2), significantly improving upon previous results.
